topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a digital slap across my already frayed nerves. 'User_Starlight' had just posted a picture of the skyline, and the caption read, "Best view in the city, seriously." My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the muscles ache. I stared at the photo, then down at my own cramped apartment, the view being a brick wall and a dumpster. It was impossible. Starlight had been vague, their location perpetually shrouded in digital mist. I typed a message, my fingers hovering over the keys, each word tasting like acid. "Where are you?" I demanded. The reply came back instantly, startling me with its casual familiarity: "Right around the corner. Running errands this morning." A cold, hard knot formed in my stomach, quickly escalating into a tight, vibrating tension. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing in the silence of my room. I needed air. I needed distance.

[story 2]

I reread the shared post for the tenth time. The angle of the sun in the photo was unmistakable; it was *our* sun. My breath hitched, shallow and fast. A deep, churning heat rose in my chest, making my palms slick against the keyboard. This couldn't be happening. Starlight, the ethereal voice across continents, was breathing the same polluted air I did. I paced the small living room, my steps quick and uneven, my shoulders hunched up around my ears. Every casual comment, every shared meme, suddenly recontextualized. It wasn't just an online acquaintance anymore; it was a proximity threat. I grabbed my phone, my thumb shaking violently as I scrolled through local news articles, searching for any thread connecting Starlight to this specific metropolis. The silence of the apartment was suffocating, punctuated only by the frantic drumming of my pulse.

[story 3]

My eyes scanned the profile picture, zooming in on the background of the photograph. That particular architectural detail—the spire of the old library—was something I recognized immediately. A sharp, almost painful realization shot through me, followed by a surge of pure, unadulterated aggravation. I leaned back in my chair, letting out a slow, controlled exhale that didn't quite reach my eyes. I felt a sudden, intense need to shatter something, anything, to release the pressure building behind my sternum. I typed a response, deliberately slow, ensuring the tone was razor-sharp. "That view? You mean *this* view?" I typed, my knuckles white against the desk. I waited, the seconds stretching into an eternity. When the response finally arrived, it was brief, a single emoji, yet it sent a shockwave through me—a violent jolt that left me leaning forward, vibrating with contained energy.
